coredata.dc:title: Magnetocaloric effect and specific heat of gapped graphene quantum dots in external fields
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2025 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Wenfang, Shi
Zhongliang, Ru
Zhang, Jie
coredata.dc:description: This study examines the effects of temperature, radius, Aharonov-Bohm (AB) flux, and gap energy on the specific heat and magnetocaloric potential of bilayer graphene quantum dots (GQDs). By establishing the continuity of eigen spinors at the GQD interface, we have an equation that reveals the reliance of energy levels on external physical parameters. The specific heat shows a Schottky anomaly, increasing with temperature, peaking, and then decreasing. Stronger magnetic fields shift the peak temperature without changing the peak value. The magnetocaloric potential increases with magnetic field strength, at higher temperatures, where thermal agitation dominates.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Gapped graphene QDs
Magnetocaloric
Specific heat
Aharonov-Bohm flux

ce:caption.ce:simple-para:
Schematic representation of a GQD with radius R, embedded in a graphene sheet under a perpendicular magnetic field B, subjected to AB flux fields
ϕ i
and an energy gap Δ .
The effect of temperature on the specific heat of GQDs under varying magnetic field.
The effect of temperature on the specific heat of GQDs under varying radii.
The effect of temperature on the specific heat of GQDs under varying Aharonov-Bohm flux.
The effect of temperature on the specific heat of GQDs under varying gap energy.
The effect of AB flux on the specific heat of GQDs under varying (a) temperature (b) magnetic field.
The effect of temperature, magnetic field, AB flux, and energy gap on the specific heat.
The effect of temperature on the magnetocaloric of GQDs under varying magnetic field.
The effect of temperature on the magnetocaloric of GQDs under varying radii.
The effect of temperature on the magnetocaloric of GQDs under varying AB flux.
The effect of temperature on the magnetocaloric of GQDs under varying gap energy.
Temperature dependence of the mean energy of a GQDs under various magnetic field strengths.
Variation of mean energy in GQDs with magnetic field at different temperatures.

head.ce:title: Magnetocaloric effect and specific heat of gapped graphene quantum dots in external fields

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: This study examines the effects of temperature, radius, Aharonov-Bohm (AB) flux, and gap energy on the specific heat and magnetocaloric potential of bilayer graphene quantum dots (GQDs). By establishing the continuity of eigen spinors at the GQD interface, we have an equation that reveals the reliance of energy levels on external physical parameters. The specific heat shows a Schottky anomaly, increasing with temperature, peaking, and then decreasing. Stronger magnetic fields shift the peak temperature without changing the peak value. The magnetocaloric potential increases with magnetic field strength, at higher temperatures, where thermal agitation dominates.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Graphene, recognized as the most significant 2D carbon allotrope, is renowned for its exceptional electrical, thermal, and mechanical properties, positioning it as a transformative material across a range of disciplines. Its applications extend from electronics, where it facilitates the development of faster, more efficient devices, to energy storage, enhancing the performance of high-capacity batteries and supercapacitors, as well as in materials science, where it strengthens composites and coatings [1–3]. However, a key limitation of graphene is its lack of a natural band gap, which hinders its use in electronic devices like transistors and logic gates [4]. Without a band gap, graphene cannot be easily switched on and off, resulting in poor signal modulation and high leakage currents, making electron flow control difficult. The challenge of creating energy gaps between 0.5 and 1 eV without defects or chemical terminations further complicates the development of graphene-based devices with suitable on–off ratios and mobilities at room temperature [4]. To address this limitation, various strategies have been proposed. One approach involves cutting graphene into nanoribbons, which induces a band gap due to quantum confinement effects. The band gap increases as the ribbon width decreases, with widths below 40 nm showing a band gap exceeding 0.1 eV [5]. However, the fabrication of nanoribbons necessitates high-resolution lithography, which can be challenging and may result in variations in the band gap due to fluctuations in ribbon size [5]. Another method involves applying an external electric field perpendicular to bilayer graphene, which can induce a band gap by breaking the symmetry between the layers. However, the required field strength for this effect is often too high for practical applications [6]. Additionally, doping graphene with elements such as nitrogen (N) and boron (B) can introduce a band gap, with N and B doping achieving band gaps of up to 0.379 eV and 0.386 eV, respectively, and co-doping reaching up to 0.712 eV [7]. Nonetheless, maintaining uniform doping concentrations remains a challenge, impacting the consistency of the band gap [7]. Mechanical strain or defects can also alter the electronic structure of graphene, potentially opening a band gap. However, achieving uniform distribution of strain or defects across the material is a critical and challenging task [8].

A band gap can be effectively induced by isolating a graphene quantum dot (GQD), which is often referred to as an “artificial atom” due to its atomic-like characteristics. GQDs are composed of small fragments of graphene, and their band gap emerges as a result of quantum confinement effects. As the size of the GQD decreases, the band gap increases, thereby enabling the tuning of its electronic properties [9,10]. Furthermore, the shape and edge structure of the GQDs, along with the introduction of doping or functionalization, can further modulate the band gap, enhancing the versatility of GQDs for a wide range of applications [11,12].

Trapping electrons in quantum dots has been a pivotal research area in condensed matter physics for several decades, and it continues to attract significant attention today, both for its fundamental insights and its potential application [13,14]. Trapping electrons in GQDs is challenging due to Klein tunneling, which allows electrons to traverse potential barriers without reflection, hindering confinement [15]. Various strategies have been proposed to enhance electron trapping, including: employing twisted light to affect electron scattering [16]; introducing a mass term in magnetic quantum dots to boost electron density and trapping times [17]; optimizing angular momentum and barrier parameters in circular GQDs [18]; and applying magnetic flux to stabilize trapped states and extend trapping durations [19]. Some works have been investigated thermodynamics properties of graphene [20–23]. All the observations argued that the magnetic field plays a crucial role. Also, the AB flux's specialized role in quantum transport and magnetism, while thermodynamic properties like specific heat remain largely unaffected [24–26].

Recently, Bouhlal et al. [9] demonstrated that, unlike graphene, which inherently lacks a natural band gap, gapped GQDs exhibit tunable electronic properties due to quantum confinement effects. Gapped graphene refers to graphene that has been modified to exhibit a non-zero electronic band gap, which is the energy difference between the valence and conduction bands. This modification is crucial for enabling graphene's use in semiconductor applications, as pristine graphene typically has no band gap, making it a conductor rather than a semiconductor. Furthermore, the energy levels of fermions within a circular GQD subjected to external magnetic and Aharonov-Bohm (AB) fields have been investigated. In this study, we extend this research by exploring, for the first time, the specific heat and magnetocaloric potential of GDQs under these conditions. An investigation is conducted for the first time into the effects of temperature, radius, AB flux, and gap energy on the specific heat and magnetocaloric potential of GQDs.

A circular GQD of radius R is considered, subjected to a combined magnetic field and AB flux oriented along the z-direction, with the field configuration given by

Now, we shall examine the situation wherein an external magnetic field is considered, accompanied by the presence of an Aharonov-Bohm flux within a quantum dot. As the intensity of the magnetic field is augmented, the degenerate energy levels corresponding to each m value experience a bifurcation, subsequently leading to the amalgamation of quantum dot states that culminate in the formation of Landau levels. Specifically, in the presence of a highly intense magnetic field, the term
r 2
2
l B 2
tends toward zero, allowing us to approximate the hypergeometric function by:
(19)
F
1 , 1
α β γ
=
Γ

e γ

γ
− 1

Utilizing the Gamma function to express binomial coefficients and applying the relation Γ
y + 1
= y Γ
y
, Eq. (19) can be simplified to
(20)

ℏ
v F
l B
2
n 2
+ 2

+
j
+ j + 1 − s
where the quantum n = 1 , 2 , 3 , … are indexing the Landau levels, and j = m − α +
Φ i
.

In the other hand, to derive thermodynamic functions, we require to calculate the partition function which is given by
(21)
Q =
∑
n = 1
10
e
− β
E n
where β = 1 /
k B
T
,
k B
is the Boltzmann constant,
E n
are energy levels. By independently introducing the expressions represented by Eq. (19) into the framework of Eq. (20), we are afforded the opportunity to derive a numerical evaluation of the partition function, which serves as a pivotal component in the realm of thermodynamic analysis. A starting point to derive thermodynamic properties of the system is the partition function. Now, with the help of the partition function Eq. (21), one can derive the thermodynamic functions as.
(i)
Mean energy U = −
∂
∂ β
ln Q .
(ii)
Specific heat
C v
= −
∂
∂ β
U
(iii)
Entropy S =
k B
T ln Q −
k B
β
∂
∂ β
ln Q

Generally, MCE is a response of magnetic materials to the action of an external magnetic field at a given temperature. This effect is an interesting ability of magnetic materials to either change their temperature or exchange heat with a thermal reservoir under a magnetic field change. In characterizing the magnetocaloric effect, two quantities apply. The first one is the magnetocaloric potential, ∆ S , and the second one is the temperature variation ∆ T . Thus, to obtain the magnetic entropy change, we first need to evaluate the entropy with and without magnetic field. For an isothermal process, the quantity ∆ S is given by:
(22)
∆ S = S
B ≠ 0
T
− S
B = 0
T

The temperature dependence of the specific heat of GQDs under varying magnetic field conditions is presented in Fig. 2 . Regardless of the magnetic field strength, the specific heat exhibits a characteristic behavior: it initially increases with rising temperature, reaches a maximum, and then begins to decrease, trending toward zero at higher temperatures. This trend reflects the thermal excitation of states within the GQD system, where the specific heat is initially dominated by low-energy excitations and later by the depletion of accessible states at elevated temperatures. As the magnetic field strength increases, the temperature at which the maximum specific heat occurs shifts to higher values, indicating that stronger magnetic fields alter the thermal response of the system by modifying the energy levels and the density of states. Specifically, for a magnetic field of 5 mT, the maximum specific heat occurs at approximately 2.5 K, while for a magnetic field of 20 mT, the maximum specific heat shifts to around 8.4 K. Notably, the maximum value of the specific heat remains unaffected by the magnetic field strength and stabilizes at approximately 0.44 J/K under both conditions. At higher temperatures, the specific heat decreases and reaches a relatively low value. For instance, at around 30 K, the specific heat is approximately 0.01 J/K for a magnetic field of 5 mT. However, for a stronger magnetic field of 50 mT, the specific heat reaches the same value at a much higher temperature of around 80 K, highlighting the influence of the magnetic field on the thermal properties of the GQD system over a broader temperature range.

The peak structure observed in the specific heat, known as the Schottky anomaly, is a distinctive thermodynamic phenomenon that arises in systems with discrete, low-lying energy levels. This anomaly reflects how thermal energy excites transitions between quantized energy states, leading to a characteristic maximum in the specific heat as a function of temperature. At very low temperatures, only the lowest energy level is populated, resulting in a small internal energy and, consequently, a low specific heat capacity. As the temperature increases, the upper energy level begins to be populated, leading to a rapid increase in internal energy and specific heat capacity. The peak in specific heat occurs when the temperature is such that both energy levels are almost equally populated. This typically happens at a temperature comparable to the energy gap between the levels. Beyond this temperature, further energy absorption is limited, and the specific heat decreases as the upper level becomes fully populated.

The temperature dependence of the specific heat of GQDs with varying radii is depicted in Fig. 3 . The results demonstrate a trend where the specific heat initially increases with temperature, reaches a maximum, and subsequently decreases, exhibiting a behavior that is consistent across different magnetic field conditions. A key observation is that as the radius of the quantum dot increases, the temperature at which the maximum specific heat occurs also shifts to higher values. This shift, however, is minimal for smaller radii. For GQDs with radii of 10 nm, 30 nm, 50 nm, and 70 nm, the maximum specific heat is observed at temperatures of 3.4 K, 3.7 K, 4.1 K, and 5.6 K, respectively. This suggests that larger quantum dots require higher temperatures to reach the peak of specific heat, likely due to the increased number of available states for thermal excitation as the radius increases. The small shift in the peak temperature for smaller radii indicates a less pronounced change in the thermodynamic response with respect to size, which could be attributed to the relatively lower energy gap between discrete energy levels in these smaller dots.

The temperature dependence of specific heat for GQDs under varying Aharonov-Bohm flux conditions is presented in Fig. 4 . The results reveal that the influence of Aharonov-Bohm flux on the specific heat behavior is minimal. The flux, which typically influences the phase coherence of quantum systems, appears to have a negligible impact on the thermal properties of the GQDs within the temperature range considered. This suggests that the specific heat response of the system is largely governed by other factors, such as temperature and quantum dot size, rather than by the Aharonov-Bohm flux, at least within the limits explored in this study.

The minimal influence of the AB flux on the specific heat of GQDs arises from the distinct physical mechanisms governing thermodynamic properties compared to electronic transport phenomena. While the AB flux modulates quantum interference in charge transport (e.g., conductance oscillations), specific heat reflects the total energy stored in the system's degrees of freedom (electronic, vibrational, or spin excitations) and is largely insensitive to phase coherence effects. This discrepancy occurs because (1) specific heat depends primarily on energy storage mechanisms (e.g., phonons or spin excitations) rather than quantum interference, (2) thermal fluctuations at finite temperatures suppress phase-sensitive contributions, and (3) magnetic field-induced energy spectrum modifications often overshadow AB flux effects. Thus, the AB flux's role is prominent in quantum transport but negligible in thermodynamic properties like specific heat [24–26].

The temperature dependence of specific heat for GQDs under varying gap energy is illustrated in Fig. 5 . At lower gap energies, the specific heat behavior of GQDs remains largely unaffected, with negligible changes observed for gap energies of 1 meV and 5 meV, where the specific heat values are nearly identical. However, as the gap energy increases, a shift in the thermal properties is noted. Specifically, the temperature at which the maximum specific heat occurs progressively decreases with increasing gap energy. This trend indicates that higher gap energies suppress the thermal excitation of charge carriers, leading to a lower temperature threshold for the peak in specific heat.

The dependence of specific heat on the AB flux for GQDs is investigated under varying temperature and magnetic field conditions, as depicted in Fig. 6 . The results reveal a clear trend: as the AB flux increases, the specific heat of GQDs decreases exponentially. This behavior can be attributed to the flux-induced modification of the electronic states within the quantum dot, which alters the thermodynamic properties of the system. At higher temperatures and low magnetic fields, the effect of the AB flux on the specific heat is minimal. This suggests that, under these conditions, the thermal energy is sufficiently high to occupy a broad range of available states, and the contribution of the AB flux to the system's thermodynamic response becomes less significant. In contrast, at lower temperatures or higher magnetic fields, the AB flux has a more pronounced effect, with a notable decrease in specific heat, reflecting a stronger influence on the energy distribution of the quantum dot's electronic states. The observed exponential decrease in specific heat with increasing AB flux can be explained by the suppression of electronic states near the Fermi level, which diminishes the density of states available for thermal excitation.

The influence of temperature, magnetic field, AB flux, and energy gap on the specific heat is illustrated in Fig. 7 . The contour plot in Fig. 7 (a) illustrates the variation of specific heat (
C v
) as a function of B and T, with distinct regions of behavior. The yellow region represents a parameter space where
C v
is relatively high (
C v
≈ 0.4 ) and remains constant over a range of B and T. For a fixed value of
C v
, two distinct paths emerge: one below the yellow region, corresponding to lower T and B values, and another above the yellow region, spanning higher T and B. The upper region covers a broader range of magnetic field and temperature combinations, indicating greater flexibility in achieving a specific
C v
compared to the confined lower region. The contour spacing further reveals that
C v
varies more rapidly at low B and T and more gradually as the system transitions through the yellow region, highlighting the interplay between thermal and magnetic effects in determining the thermodynamic properties.

The effect of temperature on the magnetocaloric potential (ΔS) of GQDs under varying magnetic fields is presented in Fig. 8 . At a fixed temperature, the magnetocaloric potential increases with the magnetic field, indicating that stronger magnetic fields enhance the entropy change associated with the magnetocaloric effect. As the temperature increases, ΔS gradually converges to a specific value of approximately −0.7J/(kg.K), regardless of the applied magnetic field. This convergence suggests a saturation behavior at higher temperatures, where thermal agitation dominates the magnetocaloric response, reducing its dependence on the magnetic field. Additionally, as the magnetic field strength increases, the magnetocaloric potential reaches this final saturated value at progressively lower temperatures, highlighting the influence of stronger magnetic fields in accelerating the thermal equilibrium of the system.

The impact of temperature on the magnetocaloric potential of GQDs for varying quantum dot radii is depicted in Fig. 9 . The results indicate that increasing the radius of the quantum dot leads to an enhancement in the magnetocaloric potential. For smaller radii, the sensitivity of the magnetocaloric potential to changes in radius is minimal. However, as the radius exceeds 15 nm, the influence of radius becomes significant. Specifically, for a radius of 20 nm, the magnetocaloric potential reaches its peak value at approximately 4 K. In contrast, for radii of 15 nm, 10 nm, and 5 nm, the magnetocaloric potential achieves its maximum at higher temperatures of around 8 K, 10 K, and 10 K, respectively. This indicates that larger quantum dots exhibit a more pronounced magnetocaloric effect at lower temperatures, while smaller quantum dots require higher temperatures to achieve their maximum magnetocaloric potential.

The temperature dependence of the magnetocaloric potential for GQDs under varying AB flux conditions is depicted in Fig. 10 . The results indicate that as the AB flux increases, the magnetocaloric potential exhibits a consistent decrease, suggesting a suppression of the magnetocaloric effect with higher AB flux values. Notably, for AB flux values of 1.5 and 2.5, the magnetocaloric potential demonstrates identical behavior across the examined temperature range.

The temperature dependence of the magnetocaloric potential for GQDs under varying gap energy is presented in Fig. 11 . The results demonstrate that the magnetocaloric potential exhibits significant sensitivity to changes in the gap energy compared to other parameters. Specifically, a decrease in the gap energy leads to an increase in the magnetocaloric potential, indicating that reducing the gap energy enhances the system's thermodynamic response. Furthermore, the magnetocaloric potential shows a higher sensitivity to variations at larger gap energy values. This behavior suggests that the influence of the gap energy is not uniform across its range, with larger gap energies having a more pronounced impact on the magnetocaloric potential.

Fig. 12 presents the variation of the mean energy of an electron confined in a GQDs as a function of temperature for different values of the applied magnetic field, under the conditions R = 10
nm ,
Φ i
= 3.5 , Δ = 1
meV . At low temperatures, the mean energy remains nearly constant, indicating minimal thermal excitation. This temperature range over which the mean energy remains unchanged becomes broader as the magnetic field increases, demonstrating enhanced magnetic confinement at low thermal energies. As the temperature increases further, the mean energy exhibits an exponential growth and gradually saturates at a specific asymptotic value, irrespective of the magnetic field strength. It is also observed that, in general, the mean energy decreases with increasing magnetic field strength, particularly at lower temperatures. However, as temperature rises, the influence of the magnetic field on the mean energy diminishes due to dominant thermal effects overpowering magnetic confinement.

Fig. 13 presents the variation of the mean energy of GQDs as a function of the applied magnetic field for various temperatures, under the fixed parameters of R = 10
nm ,
Φ i
= 3.5 , Δ = 1
meV . It is observed that the mean energy increases with temperature at all values of the magnetic field, indicating a thermal enhancement of the electronic energy levels. However, the rate of increase in mean energy diminishes at higher temperatures, resulting in smaller differences between energy values as the temperature rises. This trend suggests a saturation behavior, wherein the mean energy approaches a limiting value with increasing temperature, largely independent of the magnetic field strength. The interplay between thermal excitation and magnetic confinement appears to stabilize the energy spectrum at elevated temperatures.

This study investigated the effects of temperature, radius, AB flux, and gap energy on the specific heat and magnetocaloric potential of GQDs. Through a rigorous analytical approach to the derivation of eigen spinors, we implemented boundary conditions to formulate an equation that characterizes the energy levels as a function of the physical parameters of the quantum dot—specifically, the magnitude of the external magnetic field and the energy gap engendered by the underlying graphene substrate. In our computational investigation, we focused on exploring the effects of the Aharonov-Bohm flux within the quantum dot on the thermodynamic properties of electron localization within the confines of the quantum dot. The specific heat of GQDs exhibited a Schottky anomaly, increasing with temperature, reaching a peak, and then decreasing. Stronger magnetic fields shifted the peak temperature without altering the peak value (∼0.44 J/K). The AB flux had minimal impact on the specific heat, with a slight decrease observed at higher flux values. The magnetocaloric potential increased with magnetic field strength, saturating at −0.7 J/(kg·K) at higher temperatures, where thermal agitation dominated. Stronger fields reached saturation at lower temperatures. Larger quantum dots enhanced the magnetocaloric effect, while smaller dots showed minimal sensitivity to radius changes. The magnetocaloric potential was more sensitive to changes in gap energy and magnetic field than to radius and AB flux. It increased as the gap energy decreased, with a more pronounced effect at larger gap energies.

Shi Wenfang: Writing – review & editing, Validation, Software, Investigation. Ru Zhongliang: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Software, Investigation. Jie Zhang: Software, Methodology, Formal analysis, Conceptualization.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: Supported by Fundamental Research Program of Shanxi Province (No. 202203021221143 and 202303021211209).
